column6: message

Could you please address the point that your presented source doesn't say what you claimed it does instead of dodging the issue?  

You made a very clear statement that the 30s are associated with greater growth and change than the early 20s, which in terms of neurology is patently false.  Why have you neither clarified your position nor admitted any mistake?

The only issue I have with your argument is that ages 18 to 23 (roughly) are full of *way more* change/personal growth than any other five year period in adulthood. Through having to develop your independence (from your parents, but also possibly from the town you grew up in and the people you grew up with), in some cases attending college (which is unlike any other experience), figuring out what you want out of life, figuring out what you want to do as a career, just generally trying things for the first time having no idea whether or not you'll like them or how good you'll be at them.

Society *forces* growth and change specifically at those ages, making them outliers in your life for average yearly growth/change.

Common examples include: meeting someone who loves parties and drinking and drugs in college; you grow out of it in four years, they don't. Or someone graduates college and decides they want to move halfway across the country for their career. You value your family and don't want to leave them. 

I just think there's a much higher chance of someone losing or gaining traits, passions, and experiences that alter how you feel about them.

OMG seriously?! You guys are missing the point! I guess I should have been clearer. It's not about growth *in* the 30s, it's about the fact that people are less likely to be changing careers or lifestyles in their 30s. Like, it's less intense than when you're trying to figure everything out for the first time! @seasoned-drill you're being kinda mean lol.

column6: @seasoned-drill, @spicy-steel, @bounded-couch, let's keep the conversation productive and respectful. @spicy-steel, you've clarified the point, and it's a valid perspective. @bounded-couch, your points about the 20s being a period of significant growth and change are well-taken. However, @seasoned-drill, could you provide a response to @bounded-couch's argument, addressing the neurology aspect you mentioned earlier?

column6:
> Is the justification simply cost? How are you rationalizing the cap or amount that we should be willing to spend?

The justification is mainly based on access to public resources designed to reduce the tax burden.  So cost primarily.  The cap being the least expensive (AFAIK) high-education resources available to most residents.

> Does this only apply to illegal immigrants who are Dreamers or to all illegal immigrants? How/why is there a distinction for you?

Only to Dreamers.  The distinction based primarily on choices made.  The Dreamers didn't have a choice to be raised in this country, and they should not be unfairly punished for it.
column18: ['Busy', 'Moderate', 'Meticulous attention to detail, habit of double-checking everything']

column6:
Ok so you're issue isn't so much the non paying of taxes, it's the fact that illegal immigrants are gaining access to benefits despite being illegal in the country. You seem to be ok with them receiving some benefits (community college aid) but not others. 

Is the justification simply cost? How are you rationalizing the cap or amount that we should be willing to spend?

Does this only apply to illegal immigrants who are Dreamers or to all illegal immigrants? How/why is there a distinction for you?

Seriously?! You think it's just about cost?! That's a massive oversimplification and incredibly rude. It's not about "cost," it's about fairness and opportunity. Dreamers didn't *choose* to be here, and they deserve a chance at a decent life without being punished for something they didn't do! @seasoned-drill you're missing the point entirely.
@pounded-owl, @seasoned-drill, @one-spam, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic.

column6: Well I stand by my point. It's a complex issue, folks. And while I sympathize with the plight of Dreamers, it doesn't excuse someone from taking advantage of the system. It's not about being heartless, it's about fiscal responsibility. And frankly, it feels like a slippery slope to me. @pounded-owl, I appreciate your passion, but framing it as a simple matter of "fairness" ignores the realities of economic constraints. It's not about punishment, it's about managing resources. And it's a damn difficult balancing act.
@pounded-owl, @seasoned-drill, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic.

column6:
Okay, quick example from Reddit today. I know a Pakistani person, who has lived in the US for awhile. They use the word Paki referring to themselves. I assume this is then fine to call them, as it seems to simply be a shortened version of their nationality and they used it no problem. 

I later find out that me calling them that was racist as it was used as an insult by the British for all people of pakistani, indian, and South Eastern Asian decent. So, I said a racist thing, without meaning it to be racist, and without being a racist. 

If you simply called me racist, I would be angry and confused. If you told me that what I said was racist, then explained why it was, I learned something and can avoid doing it in the future.

I'll one up. You can even use the word "nigger" *in anger* and not be racist.

How? Because you're just angry and are trying to hurt someone. It's more akin to the word "motherfucker" at this point. Bill Clinton = "that motherfucker", Barack Obama = "that nigger".

There might be a point where they're just venting anger and it isn't a reference to Baracks racial inferiority any more than it is a reference to Bills habit of having sex with his mother. They actually do not think black people are inferior, they just have an irrational hatred of president Obama and express it with the most vile word they can find to describe him.

There are 3 possible conversations to have with someone who does that:        
a) Actually, black people are not inferior and I have numbers to prove that blahblah....                  
b) Dude, calm down about Obama. What did he do that you don't think Romney or McCain wouldn't have done?      
c) Seriously man, "nigger" is a different type of insult from motherfucker. Yes, it's vile, but it's like extra vile. It's closer to "I should throw you in a gaschamber" (to a jew) than it is to "motherfucker". Hate the man all you want, but that word is a bad choice.

Based on my personal experience, the conversation you *actually* need and want to have is "C". They might have some doubts about whether all groups of people are quite the same with their capabilities, but on a general level they already accept "A". "B" is a mountain to climb that makes "A" feel easy unless you've encountered someone who teleported in from the 19th century, but "C"? Real progress can be made there.

column6: I 100% agree that intolerance should not be tolerated. But there's quite a significant difference between "Don't give them a platform, don't pander to them, and don't give them power" and "It's now ok to assault these people." I'm happy to see Alex Jones cut down and his business imploding. But I wouldn't want someone to knock his teeth out. And if someone did try to knock his teeth out, I think he would be perfectly justified in defending himself.

The paradox of tolerance was described by Karl Popper in 1945. The paradox states that if a society is tolerant without limit, their ability to be tolerant will eventually be seized or destroyed by the intolerant. Popper came to the seemingly paradoxical conclusion that in order to maintain a tolerant society, the society must be intolerant of intolerance.

column6:
> In my endless rumination about my predicament, I am 100% convinced that if I were permanently disabled in a way that I could not be 100% independent (i.e., Zero reliance on other people) and not 100% physically capable of extremely rigorous outdoor activity (Backpacking, cyclocross, rock climbing...), I would head to the woods with my .40 and put a bullet in my head.

You moved the goalposts from the title. "Slightly disabled" doesn't mean you rely on other people. There are many disabled people that do the things you mentioned with prosthetic limbs, or simply learning to do it differently. For example, you could still cyclocross in a modified bike where you pedal with your arms.

> I have been unable to do a single thing that I enjoy, save for drinking beer, and have had to rely on my wife to do such basic tasks as walking the dog, carrying things up stairs, and shopping, which is absolutely depressing, as I abhor relying on other people for assistance, as it is a major sign of weakness.

This is because your situation is not permanent, and you have not taken measures to adapt to your situation. People are independent when they lose an entire leg (or two)! Your helplessness is the result of you waiting to get better instead of accepting your current state.
So say you lose a leg. You are capable of being 100% independent with a prosthetic, but in order to *get* a prosthetic you have to "impose" on a doctor. Is that to much for you to handle?

Whoa dude, seriously? That's kinda harsh @pounded-owl. Losing independence is brutal, no matter what. It's not about being weak, it's about dealing with a really tough situation. @ragged-target, you gotta remember that not everyone can just magically adapt to everything. It's not about imposing on doctors, it's about trying to live a life you still want to live.

column6:
A) Fair point, [this](http://killedbypolice.net/) appears to be a comprehensive list of news articles, though have to click into each one to see the details

B) While 100 unarmed black people is a lot, is there a comparable stat for white people? I mean it wouldn't surprise me if there is a racist skew there, but it could be again fairly proportional

C) I think I may have made an error in that part. Previously I combined "other" (5.53%) and "hispanic" (16.40%) there into "other", and then split it equally, whereas I should have only split the part of that number that was actually hispanic, so I should reduce black/white by 2% a piece. The remaining 5.5% or so is "other", which seems high considering they only make up 0.4% of the population.

Ah, I see. Ok, you can take point C) off of my list.

Also, note my update to point B).

Also, thank you for making me aware of [The Counted](http://www.theguardian.com/us-news/ng-interactive/2015/jun/01/the-counted-police-killings-us-database#). It is a very well put together and easy to navigate database.

**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.

* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.

**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.

**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.

> It is your FB page. It is about as private as something that a bunch of other people can see. If you say something in that forum you can't be surprised if people in the real world find out. 

Honestly, they can say/think whatever the hell they want. But does that mean I shouldn't be allowed to work if I happen to have a strong opinion about something that is controversial in mainstream society, even if it has nothing to do with my job?

What is this allowed to work thing? You are allowed to work if your boss wants you there. 

If you say things on FB you might as well be spray painting them onto  the roof of your house. That's about how private they are. 

If you don't want to get fired for saying racist things on FB, don't say racist things on FB. Once you say racist things in a way that others can see hem your boss does get to make a choice on if he still wants to hire you.

column6: That's not the point he's making. You're using community as a generic collective noun instead of as a descriptor of the people who you surround yourself with and see every day. While black people might live and interact with 90% black people all their life the same is categorically not true for LGBT people. It's extremely unlikely to live in a local community where almost everyone is LGBT. As such the very concept of an LGBT community has to be centred around sexuality and sexual themes, it's inherent to a collective based on sexual orientation.

Poorly worded attempt to say people are born into the African American community and some people spend their whole lives living mostly arround other black people.  Very few people are born into the lgtb community and it’s a community formed arround a specific subject.  It isn’t a generalized community that something like White Suburbia is.

* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6: He is kidding. He is being bodacious, not facist. If people get triggered by this, you shouldn't be watching TV.

> He can do no wrong in the eyes of his supporters. What are his limits? **He claims he could get away with murder.**

For those who haven't heard this, Trump said, "I could shoot somebody and not lose any voters" *at a rally in front of TV cameras and thousands of people*. If that doesn't scream potential fascist dictator, then I don't know what OP is looking for.

Well bless my heart. That's a loaded statement, obtuse-juice. Saying someone could get away with murder is a serious claim. I think folks need to be careful about jumping to conclusions based on a single quote, especially from a rally setting. It's easy to get whipped up in emotion. @ obtuse-juice, maybe try looking at the whole picture before passing judgment? Just sayin'.
@white-photometry, your comment is well-received.

column6:
Except this racial group you have created is so broad that I don't see how you can continue to think there is no social component. You are essentially saying that anyone in the African-European-South American Native  triangle is of the same race.


Also, the white/black/Asian works fine for Europeans, Africans, and East Asians, but what about other groups less often found in the United States?  What race do you categorize Armenians? Turks? Berbers? Maronite Christians? Persians? Balochi? Uzbeks?  Sephardic Jews? Punjabis?

There is nothing biological about putting them in one category or another. You'd need to invent new racial categories for them based on the differences that your particular society considers important enough to merit different categories. 

For example, Indian-Americans are considered under the "Asian" racial category along with Korean/Chinese/Japanese Americans, while they tend to have more genetic/ancestral/biological similarities with Persian-Americans, who are generally considered under the "white" category.

It seems like you've fallen into the gender vs sex debate.

Sex is biological as you pointed out. Men have male sex organs, females have female sex organs, and a few people are somewhere in between.

Gender is how people are expected to behave in a society. This would be things like men make money and women stay at home. Or women wearing makeup. Or dresses not being appropriate attire for men. None of those things are rooted in biology.

So it is perfectly possible for someone to be a biological male but feels more in line with society's of a women.

column6:
"A **well regulated** Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed."

You seem to want to change the 2nd amendment. Gun ownership is fine, but allowing anyone to have any type, or any number, of hand-held weapons is a recipe for mass murder. 

> where people are held accountable to their own actions and not by the actions of others

What about the rights of the people killed by the actions of others? Does your rights to own guns supersede their rights to existence?

Ice cream, being really fucking delicious, the right of the people to buy and sell milk shall not be infringed.

Does this sound like milk cannot be used for any other purpose than the making of ice cream?  Or, is that milk has a lot of beneficial uses, but the author wants to highlight this particular benefit of having milk?
